name: Fallback Authentication Narrative and Risk Framing
consequences: Immediate: Framing fallback as 'resilience & continuity' vs. 'privacy-first' vs. 'consumer convenience' shifts which stakeholders prioritize the requirement. Systemic: Resilience framing attracts procurement officers and contingency planners (who fear supplier lock-in), while privacy framing alienates incumbent operators and convenience framing weakens urgency with policymakers. Strategic: If narrative fails to resonate with AltID procurement teams by month 14, phase three will spend resources on already-lost procurement battles rather than EU-standards influence.
options: Emphasize operational resilience and continuity-of-service risk: position fallback as infrastructure redundancy that protects citizens if Apple/Google policies change or geopolitical disruption occurs, using comparable public-sector examples from banking and healthcare.

Lead with privacy and democratic accountability: frame platform-neutral access as protection against surveillance, algorithmic manipulation, and foreign control of Danish citizen authentication data, appealing to civil society and Datatilsynet.

Anchor to consumer choice and UX parity: argue that Danish citizens should have authentication options as convenient as MitID on iOS/Android, using open standards that major OS vendors must support or face procurement exclusion.
review: Controls Stakeholder Persuasion vs. Institutional Urgency. Weakness: The options assume that different narratives will each persuade distinct institutional audiences, but they do not account for the possibility that all three narratives may be dismissed as 'nice to have' if the incumbent operators successfully argue that disruption costs outweigh resilience benefits. The framing choice is only decisive if procurement decision-makers are predisposed to question supplier concentration; without that predisposition, narrative selection becomes marginal.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Standards-Compliance Target
consequences: Immediate: Building lean demonstrators (FIDO2/WebAuthn + OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS) proves platform-neutral feasibility without feature bloat; expanded feature parity attempts would consume phase-two budget and blur credibility. Systemic: A tight, standards-focused demonstrator becomes evidence for EU and Danish procurement language (eIDAS2, NSIS 'Substantial'); feature-rich demonstrators get mistaken for product roadmaps and invite scope-creep. Strategic: If demonstrators are perceived as incomplete or toy implementations, they weaken the project's claim that real alternatives exist; if they become feature-heavy, they signal that platform-neutral paths require more development investment than MitID updates, favoring incumbent inertia.
options: Minimal standards-compliant demonstrator: restrict to FIDO2/WebAuthn authentication against a test OpenID Connect provider on GrapheneOS, no push notifications, no app-store integration, documented explicitly as non-production proof-of-concept focused on interoperability and fallback logic.

Dual-demonstrator with hardware fallback: develop the mobile standards demonstrator in parallel with a USB security-token or browser-based authentication path that requires no mobile OS at all, proving that fallback can reach citizens even if mobile platforms become unavailable or untrustworthy.

Extended feature parity demonstrator: include push-notification simulation, app-store-independent distribution, biometric support on GrapheneOS, and mock integration with Danish public-sector test environments, positioning it as a near-production reference implementation.
review: Controls Technical Credibility vs. Project Scope Management. Weakness: The lean standards-compliant option sacrifices proof that fallback authentication is as practical as MitID, risking dismissal as 'theoretically sound but unusable in practice'; the hardware-fallback option adds engineering complexity and diverges from the mainstream authentication pathway most citizens would actually use; the feature-parity option bursts the budget and becomes a product roadmap, not a feasibility study. None of the options directly addresses the question of whether GrapheneOS uptake is realistic enough to anchor national fallback strategy.

name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Adversarial Risk
consequences: Immediate: Direct engagement with MitID operator and banks risks triggering defensive behavior—legal barriers to demonstrator deployment, pressure on Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to preempt the project, or public framing of fallback requirements as dangerous and unproven. Systemic: Avoiding engagement keeps the project free from incumbent veto but reduces chances of voluntary regulatory coordination; selective engagement with sympathetic banks but not MitID operator creates coalition but signals fear of head-to-head debate. Strategic: If phase three arrives without incumbent buy-in, procurement language will be framed as adversarial and subject to delay; if early engagement triggers legal or political counterattack, the project's credibility in government circles is damaged before demonstrators are complete.
options: Collaborative approach with selected early-adopter banks: engage only smaller or more security-conscious financial institutions to pilot fallback concepts, avoiding direct confrontation with MitID operator while building proof of concept through sympathetic incumbents.

Transparent multi-stakeholder working group: include MitID operator, Finanstilsynet, Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, and civil-society representatives from phase one onward, positioning fallback as a shared resilience challenge rather than competitive threat, accepting slower consensus in exchange for reduced adversarial risk.

Delayed engagement until phase three: keep the project independent and technically focused during phases one and two, then approach incumbents with finished demonstrators and standards language as fait accompli, betting that resistance is weaker when technical proof and policy traction are established.
review: Controls Coalition Openness vs. Strategic Autonomy. Weakness: The collaborative early-adopter approach assumes smaller banks want to diversify authentication without alienating the dominant MitID ecosystem, a risky assumption if market pressure favors consolidation over redundancy. The transparent multi-stakeholder model may simply give the incumbent operator a seat at the table to slow decisions without committing to change. The delayed-engagement model trades autonomy for risk: if incumbents mount a political counterattack before phase three, the project has no ally within the identity industry and must rely entirely on external policy pressure.

name: Procurement Language Anchor Point: Danish Unilateral vs. EU-Embedded Requirements
consequences: Immediate: Danish-only procurement language for AltID procurement can be written and adopted faster (months 18–22) if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has political will; EU-embedded requirements require coordination with other member states and European Commission bodies, adding 6–12 months to formalization. Systemic: Danish unilateral requirements risk being overridden if EU harmonization later favors Apple/Google-compatible approaches; EU-embedded language is slower but harder to overturn and influences standards across eIDAS2 rollout. Strategic: If the project pursues both pathways but has insufficient phase-three resources to sustain both, it must choose between securing a narrow Danish AltID win (which may not survive EU coordination) and contributing to broader EU standards that have weaker immediate Danish impact.
options: Danish-first AltID procurement mandate: draft explicit platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements into Digitaliseringsstyrelsen's next AltID procurement RFP, using the project's demonstrators and feasibility report as technical justification, bypassing EU harmonization timelines.

EU-coordinated standards contribution: engage with eIDAS2 technical working groups, European Digital Identity Wallet architecture teams, and other member states' digital identity leads to embed fallback and platform-neutrality language into European standards and implementation guidance before those standards filter back into member-state procurement.

Hybrid sequencing: pursue Danish AltID language in phase three while simultaneously submitting white papers and technical input to EU bodies, accepting that EU adoption will lag but treating EU influence as the long-term strategic win and Danish procurement as near-term validation.
review: Controls Speed vs. Durability of Procurement Impact. Weakness: The Danish-first option assumes the Danish government will adopt procurement language that creates incompatibility with EU standards if those standards later favor incumbent platforms—a political risk that may cause backtrack. The EU-coordinated option trades the likelihood of immediate AltID impact for the hope of longer-term standardization, but other member states may not share Denmark's supplier-concentration concerns, leaving the project with limited actual influence. The hybrid approach spreads resources thin and risks winning neither the quick Danish win nor meaningful EU leverage.

name: Phase Three Contingency Reallocation: Procurement Push vs. Standards and Publication Focus
consequences: Immediate: If month-16 assessment shows weak AltID procurement traction, the project must decide whether to escalate procurement engagement (adding lobbying, legal analysis, and institutional pressure) or pivot toward EU standards contribution, publication, and coalition consolidation. Systemic: Escalating procurement effort consumes remaining budget and political capital on a target that may resist fundamental change; pivoting toward standards and publication shifts to longer-term cultural change but abandons the project's original objective of influencing AltID before it hardens. Strategic: The decision point at month 16 is binary and irreversible: resources spent on procurement cannot fund EU standards work, and vice versa. Misreading institutional appetite for change at month 16 results in wasted phase-three spending on either a lost procurement battle or consolation-prize publication work that does not move the core objective.
options: Escalate procurement engagement: if AltID stakeholders show conditional interest but require deeper technical validation or political support, commit phase-three resources to focused institutional advocacy, working-group participation, and white-paper production specifically aimed at Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and AltID procurement teams, accepting that success is uncertain.

Pivot to EU standards and publication: if Danish AltID traction is weak by month 16, reorient phase three toward sustained engagement with European Digital Identity Wallet technical groups, publication of peer-reviewed security analysis, and coalition consolidation across other EU member states and civil-society networks, treating Denmark as a pilot case rather than the primary target.

Balanced hybrid contingency: allocate phase-three budget into three roughly equal parts—continued AltID engagement, EU standards work, and high-impact publication—accepting lower intensity on all fronts but hedging against mis-prediction of which pathway will yield strategic return.
review: Controls Focus vs. Risk Mitigation at Decision Inflection Point. Weakness: The escalation option assumes that deeper advocacy will overcome institutional inertia, but procurement resistance may stem from incumbent pressure or cost-benefit skepticism that additional white papers cannot address. The pivot option abandons the original objective and requires the project to reframe success as 'influencing EU standards,' a goalpost shift that may demoralize the coalition. The balanced hybrid option avoids hard choices and spreads already-scarce phase-three resources (3.5 million DKK) across three competing demands, risking that none receives sufficient depth to be credible.

name: Coalition Composition Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Insider-heavy coalitions (government, MitID operators, telcos) gain faster access to procurement discussions but lose critical credibility with civil-society, privacy advocates, and Folketinget skeptics → Systemic: Capture risk intensifies; incumbent operators shape the 'platform-neutral' framing to mean token compatibility without genuine fallback, reducing sovereignty gains by 60-80% → Strategic: Formal policy language is weakened; phase-three procurement document includes supplier-concentration language that lacks enforcement teeth or mandatory fallback testing.
options: Lead with institutional insiders (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, financial sector) and add civil-society representation only after securing preliminary government buy-in; prioritize speed of institutional entry over public legitimacy.

Build a publicly visible coalition spanning academic security researchers, privacy NGOs (PROSA, Digitaliseringsbetaling), open-source communities, and sympathetic Folketinget members first, then approach government with demonstrated external credibility and media attention.

Create a dual-track coalition: a formal institutional advisory board with government and operator representatives isolated from public-facing strategy, paired with an independent coalition council (academics, advocates, security experts) that publishes autonomous assessments and maintains public pressure independent of government response.
review: Controls Insider Access vs. Outsider Independence. Weakness: The options fail to explicitly address how to prevent captured insiders from co-opting language; they assume external credibility alone prevents institutional neutralization, but do not specify contractual or publishing safeguards that prevent insider coalition members from silencing or softening the independent council's findings.

name: Demonstrator Architecture Scope
consequences: Immediate: Conservative demonstrator (simulated MitID environment, standard FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS) produces credible proof-of-concept but risks appearing as incremental—incumbent operators dismiss it as 'academic toy' without real-world resilience → Systemic: Phase-three procurement language becomes defensible but unambitiously framed as 'optional interoperability layer' rather than mandatory fallback → Strategic: Procurement document gains inclusion but as non-binding recommendation; actual AltID architecture remains Apple/Google-centric with token-based 'optionality' that few users adopt.
options: Demonstrate standards-based authentication (FIDO2, OpenID Connect) on GrapheneOS against a simulated public-sector identity provider; keep scope lean and certification-neutral; emphasize proof-of-concept rather than production-ready claims.

Build demonstrators spanning three fallback pathways: (1) privacy-respecting Android (GrapheneOS), (2) hardware security token (FIDO2 USB-C dongle) with browser-based login, (3) decentralized identity model (W3C Verifiable Credentials) authenticated via offline-capable QR codes or NFC; show architectural diversity that forces procurement to choose explicitly rather than defaulting to mobile-only.

Develop an end-to-end prototype that integrates with a live (sandboxed) subset of Danish public-sector identity infrastructure, demonstrating authentication against a test instance of a real government agency's identity provider; accept higher engineering risk and longer timeline in exchange for undeniable production-relevance and operator resistance.
review: Controls Technical Conservatism vs. Radical Sovereignty. Weakness: The radical option (live integration with real government infrastructure) increases timeline risk and may violate MitID or eIDAS compliance rules before formal requirements exist, exposing phase-two deliverables to legal challenge or shutdown; the options do not address how to manage regulatory risk if a demonstrator is deemed an unauthorized identity system.

name: Standards Engagement Scope and Venue
consequences: Immediate: Denmark-only engagement with eIDAS/NSIS bodies (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, Danish Standards Association) produces fast policy feedback but limits negotiating power; EU bodies treat Danish input as parochial rather than aligned with continent-wide resilience concerns → Systemic: Phase-three language reflects only Danish-specific supplier risk, not broader EU lock-in; EU-level Digital Identity Wallet working groups continue defaulting to proprietary APIs and Apple/Google-mediated trust without Danish evidence challenging that assumption → Strategic: Platform vendors successfully argue 'only Apple/Google provide sufficient device-integrity assurance for EU-mandated Substantial-level authentication'; Danish procurement language is orphaned and seen as outlier.
options: Focus exclusively on Danish institutional channels (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID governance, Finanstilsynet for banking regulation); position fallback-path proposal as domestic resilience requirement; defer EU engagement to post-phase-three if Danish traction is achieved.

Parallel-track Danish and EU engagement: submit the feasibility report and demonstrator findings to the EU Digital Identity Wallet technical working group, ENISA, and eIDAS working groups by month 18; position platform-neutral requirements as compatible with EU-mandated assurance levels and interoperability directives, converting Danish case into broader European precedent.

Lead a formal initiative within the EU Digital Governance Forum or coordinate with sympathetic EU member states (Estonia, Sweden, Netherlands) known for digital-sovereignty positions; co-author a joint position paper arguing that eIDAS2 Substantial-level assurance does not require exclusive reliance on proprietary platform integrity APIs; amplify Danish demonstrators as proof that EU-standard authentication works without Apple/Google monopoly.
review: Controls Local Influence vs. EU-Level Leverage. Weakness: The EU-engagement options assume that EU bodies will genuinely listen to technical evidence and member-state input, but do not account for the reality that EU procurement decisions are often captured by vendors with greater lobbying budgets; positioning as 'proof that alternatives work' is insufficient if no EU body is incentivized to enforce platform-neutral requirements in procurement guidance or procurement directives.

name: Fallback-Path Governance and Activation Model
consequences: Immediate: Fallback authentication designed as manual opt-in (citizens choose to use browser-token method when app-based MitID fails) appears lower-friction to incumbent operators and gains procurement traction → Systemic: Actual adoption remains <5% because default is always app-based; when platform-policy changes or geopolitical crisis occurs, fallback path is unknown to 95% of users and undertested under real-time load; continuity-of-service claims are undermined by evidence that fallback is unused → Strategic: Project is retrospectively deemed ineffective; future procurement reverts to 'maximize platform security' rather than mandate fallback testing; Danish resilience gap remains unaddressed.
options: Design fallback authentication as opt-in citizen choice with clear in-app signposting and periodic education campaigns; fallback path (browser-token, hardware token) is available but not mandatory; assume widespread adoption through awareness and convenience.

Mandate that fallback authentication must be tested and validated for 10% of critical public-sector transaction volume during each calendar year; require government agencies to demonstrate fallback uptime/latency metrics as part of annual digital-infrastructure reporting; make fallback-path maturity a KPI for identity-system certification renewal.

Implement a 'fallback-first' architecture for all non-real-time transactions: government platforms default to hardware-token or browser-based FIDO2 authentication for low-sensitivity interactions (document access, status checks, historical records), reserving app-based push authentication only for high-sensitivity transactions (fund transfers, legal declarations); citizens experience fallback as primary path, not emergency mode.
review: Controls Ease-of-Use vs. Resilience-by-Default. Weakness: The fallback-first option (radical approach) creates a bifurcated user experience that contradicts convenience arguments and may increase support costs; it also assumes that categorizing transactions into 'low/high sensitivity' is politically feasible when incumbent operators argue that all government services deserve maximum authentication assurance—the options do not address how to overcome this institutional resistance.

name: Phase-Three Contingency Trigger and Scope Adjustment
consequences: Immediate: If AltID procurement traction remains weak by month 16 (no formal response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, no procurement language drafted), continuing phase three as planned (months 17–24) risks 3.5M DKK on futile lobbying and relationship-building that produces no policy outcome → Systemic: Project concludes with published reports and EU engagement but zero inclusion in Danish procurement; successor project or policy-reform initiative must start from scratch on AltID integration, delaying fallback-path implementation to 2028–2030 → Strategic: Digital identity stack hardens; MitID/AltID architecture is locked in; project is retrospectively categorized as academic exercise rather than policy intervention; political will for resilience-driven procurement erodes as cost-minimization arguments dominate post-2026.
options: Continue phase three as planned regardless of phase-two outcomes; assume that demonstrators and published reports will eventually influence AltID even if early engagement is slow; accept risk that 3.5M DKK is spent on relationship-building and standards work with no formal procurement traction.

Establish a formal contingency gate at month 16: if Digitaliseringsstyrelsen has not issued a written response to phase-one policy proposal and no AltID procurement document mentions platform-neutrality or fallback requirements, de-scope phase three; redirect 2.0M DKK to publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-level standards input (ENISA, eIDAS working groups); reserve 1.5M DKK for a follow-on phase (months 24–36) focused on post-AltID policy reform or successor-system requirements.

Establish a mid-project trigger (month 14) for contingency assessment: commission an independent policy analyst to interview Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operators, and AltID governance stakeholders to assess genuine openness to platform-neutrality requirements; if analyst judges receptiveness as <30%, immediately initiate phase-three de-scope and issue a public statement explaining that institutional resistance prevents procurement-driven intervention, shifting strategy to long-term EU-level advocacy and next-generation identity-system framing.
review: Controls Optimism vs. Realism. Weakness: The contingency triggers (month 16 or month 14) rely on qualitative assessment of 'institutional receptiveness' and 'formal response' that may be gamed—government bodies can issue token acknowledgments without genuine commitment; the options lack hard metrics (e.g., 'procurement RFP explicitly names platform-neutrality in functional requirements') that would definitively trigger de-scope, leaving phase-three decision-making subject to interpretation and political pressure.

name: Trust Model Architecture Pathway
consequences: Immediate: Choosing cryptographic fallback (FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS) versus hardware-token-based authentication determines whether demonstrators remain OS-dependent or achieve true platform neutrality. Systemic: Platform-neutral architectures require separate certification regimes, adding 8–12 months to procurement timelines and increasing institutional overhead by 40%, but eliminate geopolitical lock-in vectors. Strategic: Selecting hardware-token fallback positions Denmark as first EU nation with portable digital identity, attracting EU standardization influence; cryptographic-only fallback reduces sovereignty gains but accelerates AltID integration likelihood.
options: Cryptographic multi-platform approach using FIDO2/WebAuthn on open-source Android and standards-based OpenID Connect, retaining some mobile OS dependency but achieving portability across Android vendors

Hardware security token (smartcard or USB-based FIDO2) with browser-native or Linux authentication path, eliminating mobile OS mediation entirely but requiring citizen hardware acquisition and enrollment overhead

Hybrid cascading model with GrapheneOS as primary fallback and hardware token as contingency-only path, balancing resilience (dual failure modes) against complexity and procurement fragmentation across three distinct trust anchors
Consequences, Review, Options
review: Controls Platform Dependency vs. Citizen Friction. Weakness: The options inadequately address cost-per-citizen for hardware distribution; neither discusses whether fallback tokens are issued proactively (citizenship baseline) or reactively (only for MitID-exclusion cases), which determines scalability economics and adoption trajectory.

name: Institutional Engagement Sequencing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Approaching Digitaliseringsstyrelsen pre-phase-two versus post-demonstrators determines whether feasibility report is abstract risk analysis or backed by working code. Systemic: Early engagement risks requirements creep (stakeholders demand feature parity with MitID before commitment) consuming 15–20% of phase-two budget; late engagement (month 14+) compresses decision-making and reduces likelihood of AltID procurement language inclusion. Strategic: Sequencing to demonstrate first, then negotiate, increases institutional credibility and reduces incumbent operator ability to dismiss platform-neutral models as theoretical.
options: Pre-demonstrator formal proposal in month 4, positioning the project as a peer to existing MitID and AltID planning cycles, accepting risk of scope inflation but securing stakeholder alignment before technical decisions lock in

Demonstrator-first engagement in month 12, publishing working code before formal procurement input, reducing political negotiation but accepting risk that AltID procurement cycle accelerates without platform-neutral requirements already embedded

Parallel institutional track with month-6 exploratory briefing to regulatory committee members, month-12 formal proposal after initial demonstrator work, and month-18 standards-working-group participation, spreading engagement risk across multiple decision gates
review: Controls Early Political Traction vs. Technical Credibility. Weakness: None of the options account for the personal turnover cycle in Danish government (ministerial reshuffles, committee rotations); engagement sequencing that assumes consistent stakeholder continuity across 24 months is brittle against electoral or administrative change.

name: Coalition Narrative Framing Posture
consequences: Immediate: Framing the issue as sovereignty/resilience versus portraying it as privacy/anti-monopoly determines which stakeholder clusters (procurement officers, cybersecurity councils, EU bodies) perceive urgency. Systemic: Sovereignty framing attracts government procurement and continuity-planning budgets but invites nationalist backlash and Apple/Google diplomatic pressure; privacy framing mobilizes civil society but deprioritizes institutional decision-makers. Strategic: Dual-track framing (sovereignty for government, resilience for operators, privacy for civil society) increases coalition reach but requires sophisticated messaging discipline and risks appearing incoherent if messaging coordination breaks down.
options: Public-sector resilience and supplier-concentration focus, positioning fallback authentication as contingency planning equivalent to backup power or redundant network infrastructure, deprioritizing privacy language and accepting civil-society organizations as secondary coalition members

Democratic accountability and privacy-centric narrative, emphasizing citizen data minimization and independence from surveillance-capable platform vendors, prioritizing civil society and academic partners but accepting lower institutional procurement influence

Parallel institutional and civil-society messaging: resilience/continuity for government stakeholders, privacy/sovereignty for citizen advocacy partners, and interoperability/standards for EU regulatory engagement, requiring separate communication channels and risking strategic coherence if messaging diverges
review: Controls Institutional vs. Citizen Legitimacy. Weakness: The options fail to specify who translates between audiences or validates message consistency; absent a dedicated communications role, divergent framing across coalition members will undermine credibility when stakeholders compare narratives.

name: Technical Demonstrator Scope and Certification Posture
consequences: Immediate: Choosing non-certified reference implementation versus pursuing interim eIDAS 'Substantial' certification on demonstrators determines credibility ceiling; certified demonstrators require 3–5 month security review and cost 800K–1.2M DKK. Systemic: Non-certified demonstrators satisfy phase-two learning goals but cannot influence procurement language without third-party security validation; certification pursuit risks phase-two delays and budget overrun if reviewers demand architecture changes. Strategic: Pursuing limited certification (e.g., OWASP SAMM Level 2 assessment or Danish Standards Foundation review) balances credibility and timeline; full eIDAS Substantial certification is unrealistic within phase two but partial external validation reframes demonstrators from 'proof-of-concept' to 'security-assessed reference design.'
options: Non-certified research-grade demonstrators with only internal security review and academic publication, maintaining aggressive timeline and budget but accepting that procurement language will reference demonstrators as 'exploratory technical work' rather than validated designs

External security assessment via accredited Danish or EU body (not full eIDAS certification), targeting OWASP SAMM Level 2 or equivalent confidence, consuming 300K DKK and 2–3 months but producing third-party validation that demonstrators meet baseline security standards

Pursue interim eIDAS Substantial certification pathway for one demonstrator (mobile-first FIDO2 implementation), treating certification process itself as policy learning opportunity and signaling institutional commitment to standards compliance, budgeting 1.2M DKK and accepting 4-month timeline impact
review: Controls Speed vs. Institutional Credibility. Weakness: The options do not distinguish between security review (technical assessment) and regulatory certification (formal authority approval); pursuing 'certification' when budget and timeline allow only review invites credibility collapse if institutional stakeholders interpret assessment as authority endorsement rather than expert opinion.

name: EU Standards Engagement Tier and Timing
consequences: Immediate: Entering EU Digital Identity Wallet working groups in month 6 versus month 18 determines whether Danish input shapes pre-final drafts or only comments on near-finalized specifications. Systemic: Early EU engagement (month 6–10) requires sustained presence and travel budget, involving 2–3 FTE weeks/month in Brussels; late engagement (month 18+) is lower cost but influences only implementation guidance and non-binding recommendations. Strategic: Timing to coincide with European Digital Identity Wallet public consultation windows (typically months 8–10 and 16–18) maximizes leverage; Danish platform-neutrality language inserted in official EU consultation responses carries weight if backed by working demonstrators and formal AltID proposal.
options: Focused Danish advocacy within eIDAS working groups starting month 6, positioning Denmark as EU pilot jurisdiction for platform-neutral identity access, accepting high coordination overhead but securing influence on normative EU language before final specifications

Publication-and-comment strategy: release phase-two demonstrators and feasibility findings as open-source and present to EU bodies in month 18, during implementation guidance phase, allowing peer adoption without formal working-group participation commitment

Collaborative EU standards pathway with selected partners (e.g., German BSI, French ANSSI equivalents) in months 10–16, co-authoring position papers on interoperability and fallback access before final consultation closes, balancing Danish advocacy with broader European legitimacy and shared resource costs
review: Controls Danish Leadership vs. Resource Sustainability. Weakness: None of the options account for the fact that EU working-group participation requires sustained technical presence (not one-off advocacy); absent dedicated rotation within the project team or external partnerships providing continuity, Danish input will fade after initial contributions, reducing long-term EU influence.
